page: Google Cloud Platform n4a-highmem-2 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine belonging to the n4a server family. It features 2 dedicated vCPUs paired with 16.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. This instance does not include local storage or integrated GPU hardware, relying entirely on network-attached storage for boot volumes and data persistence. Additionally, it does not come with complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Operating with dedicated CPU allocation, the instance provides predictable compute performance without the resource contention typical of shared-core or burstable configurations. The 1:8 vCPU-to-RAM ratio positions this server as a cost-efficient option for workloads requiring moderate processing power but a larger memory footprint. By avoiding the overhead of bundled local NVMe storage or GPU accelerators, resources are optimized strictly for memory-dense, non-accelerated applications.

The hardware configuration is suited for memory-sensitive workloads that do not require high-performance local disk I/O or parallel GPU processing. Suitable use cases include small-scale databases, in-memory caching layers, enterprise applications with moderate user concurrency, and lightweight data processing pipelines.
description: Google Cloud Platform n4a-highmem-2 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine within the n4a family, configured with 2 dedicated vCPUs and 16.0 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU, making it suitable for memory-intensive tasks that require predictable compute performance without the need for local storage or GPU acceleration. The instance features no bundled local storage or complimentary public IPv4 addresses, relying on network-attached storage for its operations. With its dedicated CPU allocation, the n4a-highmem-2 avoids the performance variability of burstable instances. It is designed for workloads such as small-scale databases, in-memory caching, and lightweight enterprise applications that benefit from a larger memory footprint relative to compute capacity.
